explanation: This bypass uses string concatenation to break up the 'Remove-MpPreference' keyword. Since the Sigma rule uses a simple 'contains' check on the CommandLine, it fails to match when the cmdlet name is not present as a single contiguous string in the process creation logs.

This approach utilizes PowerShell Aliasing. By renaming the cmdlet to 'RMP' within the session, the actual execution command line recorded by the OS does not contain the literal string 'Remove-MpPreference', thus bypassing the selection_remove filter.

The Sigma rule targets process creation logs via 'CommandLine|contains'. By using Base64 encoding (-EncodedCommand), the sensitive strings are masked from simple pattern matching filters that do not perform automatic decoding of PowerShell arguments.

This bypass targets the 'selection_tamper' part of the rule. By using a splatting technique with concatenated dictionary keys, the literal string '-ControlledFolderAccessProtectedFolders ' (including the trailing space or specific hyphenation) is never explicitly present in the command line string.
